raw_recent_text: "Ugh, not again," you spout as the old wagon shutters, then dies.
Stranded on the road again, but this time, your money has gone dry.
All but your 50 dollar bill that Grandma gave you on her last Christmas.
You've been saving it for when you needed it most, but surely, it won't be enough.
The door creaks open, and you step out into the night sky, coughing up the smoke from your fried vehicle.
After pushing your car off the road and between the trees, there isn't much else left for you to do,
so you begin to wander down the dark, lonely street.
But at the end of the road, where concrete turned to stone turned to dirt, you notice a light up ahead, on the top of a hill.
As you waltz into the old, wooden shack, your eyes begin to light up with the fire of a thousand suns.
Roulette wheels! Poker tables! And in a dark corner of the abandoned casino, sits a dealer, shuffling cards for a new round of Blackjack.
That 50 dollars might just come in handy after all. Thanks, Grandma!
As you go to sit down at the table, you hear the Dealer cough, then watch as he sits up.
In a deep, and yet strained voice, the Dealer, cloaked in darkness, poses a question to you.
Would you like to play a game of Blackjack?
You have $50

The dream is about water. Rising water. You're in the car and it's filling up. First your feet. Then your lap. Then your chest.
You can't open the doors. The windows are locked. The water is warm, which is somehow worse.
You gasp awake. Bone dry. Heart hammering. You open the car door just to prove you can.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Another night. Another dance with chance.

with matching black hat and boots, and a short brown beard.
He's riding a magnificent horse, muscular, but nimble, each step powerful, but precise.
The man reaches your window, and in a deep southern accent, he begins to talk to you.
"Howdy, partner! The name's Jameson. Davey Jameson.
"I happen to notice you were admiring my steed. He's a beauty, isn't he. "
"You see, it's common courtesy when a cowboy rides by, "
to give their mighty steed a carrot, as a way to express your gratitude for their hardwork and commitment to the job."
"You my friend, you are carrotless. That's quite a disrespectful showing towards my steed. "
"My, my, this can't do at all. What if another cowboy comes by, you're just gonna disrespect their steed, too? "
"Tell you what, I happen to have one spare carrot in my pouch. Take this, and be ready. "
"You never know when a cowboy's gonna come trotting by."
Davey Jameson hands you his Carrot, and smiles.
"See, with this carrot in your possession, you're ready for anytime a cowboy strolls on down this road. "
"Just give their steed a carrot, and they'll be very grateful."
And with that, Jameson reins his horse high into the air, gives you a yee-haw, then dashes off down the road.
Your wagon isn't road-ready, but there are still places you can reach before sundown.
Would you like to spend your day driving somewhere?

"You. You're awake. Good. You know that you aren't supposed to be here? "

As you're walking along the side of the road, something catches your eye. A flash of white, darting between the bushes. A rabbit!
Without thinking, you give chase. The little creature bounds ahead of you, zigging and zagging through the underbrush with seemingly effortless grace.
You run and run, but no matter how fast you go, the rabbit stays just out of reach. Its white tail bobs mockingly in the moonlight.
Finally, you stop, hands on your knees, gasping for breath. When you look up, the rabbit is gone, vanished into the night like it was never there.
You trudge back to your wagon, defeated. But something tells you this isn't the last time you'll see that rabbit.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
Still alive? Good. The cards would be disappointed otherwise.

raw_recent_text: Woo-hoo!
You've survived 15 days!
Yesterday, at this time, you had $24.
Since then, you've managed to lose $6.
That brings you to a grand total of $18!
Hey, at least you can afford... some things. Not many things. But some.
Your efforts haven't gone unnoticed. They're just not always mentioned.
If you ever feel lost, just remember:

"Every form has its own meaning. Every man creates his meaning and form and goal. Why is it so important - what others have done? Why does it become sacred by the mere fact of not being your own? Why is anyone and everyone right - so long as it's not yourself? Why does the number of those others take the place of truth? Why is truth made a mere matter of arithmetic - and only of addition at that? Why is everything twisted out of all sense to fit everything else? There must be some reason. I don't know. I've never known it. I'd like to understand."

You wake up smiling. When's the last time that happened? You can't remember, but it happened today.
You hear a knock on your window. Cop knock. The heavy, authoritative kind.
You sit up and see a police officer standing outside your car. He's stocky, late 40s,
with a thick mustache and a name tag that reads MARTINEZ.
"License and registration, please."
You fumble around. You don't actually have a registration. You're not even sure where your license is.
"License and registration, please."
You fumble around. You don't actually have a registration. You're not even sure where your license is.
You hand over a crumpled fast food receipt and hope for the best.
Martinez stares at it. Stares at you. Back at the receipt.
"This is a receipt for a cheeseburger."
"...It was a good cheeseburger."
Martinez sighs the sigh of a man who has seen everything.
"I'm going to pretend this didn't happen. Don't make me regret it."
He taps the roof of your car twice, gets back in his cruiser, and drives off.
Your knee has seen better days. Much, much better days.

You wake up refreshed and optimistic. The optimism will probably wear off by noon, but for now, you bask in it.
It's late. You're heading back to your car when you spot a figure sitting behind the convenience store,
leaning against the dumpster. It's Kyle.
He's got a cigarette in one hand and a crumpled piece of paper in the other.
He doesn't notice you at first.
When he does, he jumps.
"Jesus! You scared me. What are you doing back here?"
You nod at the paper. It's a hospital bill. The number at the bottom has five digits.
Kyle looks at the bill, then at you.
"You wouldn't understand. Nobody does. I've been... taking money. From the register. "
"Small amounts. For my mom. She needs it more than Hendricks does."
Kyle looks at you with desperate eyes.
"You're not gonna tell anyone, right?"

Except you can't move.
Not paralyzed-numb. Paralyzed-trapped. Your body is awake but it won't listen.
Your arms are dead weight. Your legs won't respond. You can't even turn your head.
Then you see it. In the corner of your vision. Something in the back seat.
A shape. Darker than dark. It doesn't have edges — it has suggestions of edges.
Like smoke that decided to sit down.
It's watching you. You can feel it watching you even though it doesn't have eyes.
Your breathing gets faster. Your heart is hammering. But you. Can't. Move.
The shape leans forward. Closer. You can feel cold radiating off it like an open freezer.
Then — snap — your body releases. You bolt upright, gasping.
The back seat is empty. Nothing there. Just the dark.
You don't sleep for the rest of the night. You sit with every light on and your eyes open,
watching the back seat in the rearview mirror.
Nothing comes. But the cold lingers.
Your knee has seen better days. Much, much better days.

He has a doctor's bag — the old-fashioned kind, black leather — and a smile that belongs
on a used car salesman.
"You look like a person in pain. I'm a person who fixes pain."
He opens his bag. Inside are rows and rows of pill bottles, neatly organized.
"Name's Dr. Feelgood. Not my real name, obviously. But nobody's using their real name ""out here, are they?"
He holds up a single white pill between his thumb and forefinger.
"First one's free. That's not a gimmick. I genuinely want to help. ""Think of it as a... demonstration."
"Smart. Or stupid. Time will tell."
He shrugs and walks away, humming. The bag full of pills clinking softly.

You slept like a baby. A big, grown baby in a car, but a baby nonetheless.
Kyle flags you down the moment you walk in. The store is empty. He looks wired. Jittery.
"Hey. Hey. Close the door."
He pulls out a wad of cash from under the counter. It's not small anymore.
This is hundreds of dollars.
"Hendricks still hasn't noticed. He's got three stores, he barely checks the books. "
"I've been taking more. A lot more. Mom's doing better, she's got this new treatment..."
He pushes some bills toward you.
"Here. Take some. You've kept your mouth shut. You deserve it. Call it... ""a loyalty bonus."
"There's "$200" on the counter."
"No? Fine. More for mom."
He shoves the cash back under the counter. His hands are shaking again,
but not from sadness this time. He's excited. That's worse.
You leave with a bad feeling.
Something's definitely wrong with your body. That's for certain.

You had a dream about winning at Blackjack. Then you woke up. But you still feel great.
You're looking for a dropped quarter under the driver's seat when your hand
touches something cold and hard. Metal.
You pull it out. It's a lockbox. A real, proper lockbox. Steel. Heavy. About the size
of a shoebox. There's a keyhole on the front and a combination dial on top.
It's padlocked shut with a serious-looking Master lock.
You shake it. Something slides around inside. Multiple somethings.
They clink against each other like glass or metal.
This wasn't here yesterday. You'd have noticed. Which means someone put it
in your car while you were sleeping.
On the bottom, scratched into the metal with something sharp:
"DON'T OPEN UNTIL READY"
On the bottom, scratched into the metal with something sharp:
"DON'T OPEN UNTIL READY"
Probably wise. You don't know what's in there. Could be valuables.
Could be evidence. Could be somebody's ashes.
Could be a lot of things.
You put the lockbox in your trunk. You need to find whoever left it —
or at least find the key.

raw_recent_text: Oh, look at you, making small changes like you're actually going to finish something. Adorable.

"Never do anything half-heartedly; always use your full heart, even if it's misguided, wrong, or downright bizarre. Full-hearted mistakes make the best stories."
Press a key to continue: ~ ~ ~ Morning, Day 33 ~ ~ ~  True
Your current sanity: 41%
You slept so hard you forgot where you were for a second. That's the good kind of confusion.
You've been fighting sleep for days. Coffee. Slapping yourself. Driving with the windows down.
It doesn't matter. It comes when it wants to.
This time, you don't even realize you've fallen asleep. One moment you're driving.
The next, you're parked somewhere you don't recognize, engine off, and you can't move.
The shadow fills the entire car. It's everywhere. In the mirrors, in the corners,
in the gaps between the seats.
"I'm not what you think I am."
"I'm not a monster. I'm a door. Walk through me and the pain stops. The cold stops. The hunger, the loneliness, the endless, stupid cards — all of it. Gone."
"I've been watching you lose for so long. I'm offering you the only guaranteed win."
The cold is so intense your teeth are chattering. Or trying to — your jaw is locked.
Inside your head, you hear the question clearly:
"Do you accept?"
No.
Not a thought. A word. Your lips move even though your body is frozen.
You force it out through locked teeth and a locked jaw.
"No."
The shadow recoils. The cold retreats. For the first time,
you hear something from it that isn't confidence.
"...Interesting."
Your body releases. You sit up. The car is normal. Warm. Silent.
But in the rearview mirror, just for a second, you see it retreat into the shadows
behind your headrest. And you could swear it's smiling.
Your leg is broken. It just is. Has to be.
Your wagon isn't road-ready, but there are still places you can reach before sundown.
Would you like to spend your day driving somewhere?
1. Doctor's Office
2. Stay Home

"Let's embark on a journey, a meandering path not unlike the serpentine wanderings of a leaf caught in a capricious autumn breeze, which, as it dances to the silent music of nature, reminds us of the unpredictable choreography of existence. This leaf, let's call it Gerald, flutters with an elegance borne of happenstance, a fragile vessel for the whims of the wind. Gerald's journey is not linear, nor is it bound by the rigid expectations of society's ceaselessly grinding gears. Instead, Gerald twirls, dips, and soars, embracing the chaos with a grace we can only aspire to. Now, consider the ant, industrious and steadfast, a creature of purpose and communal toil. Our ant, whom we'll name Beatrice, marches diligently on her quest for sustenance, her life a testament to the virtues of hard work and persistence. Yet, in the grand tapestry of the cosmos, what is Beatrice but a speck, a mere blip on the infinite canvas of the universe? And yet, does her insignificance in the face of the vast unknown render her efforts moot? I posit that it does not, for in the grand scheme, all actions, large and small, contribute to the intricate mosaic of existence. But back to Gerald, who by now has traversed the convoluted landscapes of our imagination, touching upon the existential questions that haunt the periphery of our consciousness. What can we learn from Gerald and Beatrice? Is it their resilience, their unyielding will to persist in the face of the insurmountable odds stacked against them by the very nature of their existence? Or is it, perhaps, the simple beauty of their dance, a reminder that life, in all its complexity and confusion, offers moments of sublime beauty, fleeting and precious, to be cherished and remembered? As Gerald finally comes to rest upon the earth, joining Beatrice in the eternal cycle of life and death, we are reminded that all journeys, whether they be of leaves or ants or the human heart, are interconnected in the grand, bewildering dance of the cosmos. So, what was the point of this story, you may ask? Well, that, my friend, is entirely up to you."

That might have been the best sleep of your life. You feel incredible.
You're at the gas station filling up — or rather, pretending to while you use the bathroom —
when you notice a man sitting at the bus stop across the street.
No striped shirt. No white paint. Just a regular guy in a brown jacket.
But you recognize the way he sits. The way he holds his hands. The way he watches everything.
That's the mime. That's Gerald.
You walk over. He sees you coming and tenses up, like he might run.
"Please. Sit down. Don't make it obvious."
His voice is quiet. Raspy. Like he hasn't used it in a long time.
"I was a performer. A real one. Theaters, festivals, the whole thing. "
"Then I saw something I shouldn't have. Backstage at a casino. Money changing hands. "
"Important hands. The kind of hands that make people disappear."
"The mime thing started as a disguise. Turns out, nobody looks twice at a mime. "
"You're invisible. The perfect hiding spot is where everyone can see you."
He glances around nervously.
"I've been watching you because you're in their territory. The gambling road. "
"They don't like people winning too much. Drawing attention."
He walks away. No moonwalk this time. Just a scared man in a brown jacket.
Looking at your skinned knee, you swear you can see the bone.
Your wagon isn't road-ready, but there are still places you can reach before sundown.
